topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy dropped onto a wooden desk. Professor Albright was leaving. It wasn't a dramatic announcement; just a brief, polite notification of his departure for a new post. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent lights of the library suddenly feeling too bright, too sterile. My hands, resting on the keyboard, began to tremble almost imperceptibly. I reread the words three times, searching for some hidden clause, some indication that this was a mistake, a temporary arrangement. Nothing. I closed the laptop slowly, the click of the lid sounding deafening in the quiet study room. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum echoing my own internal deflation. The stack of annotated drafts beside me seemed suddenly monumental, a vast, unmanageable mountain I was now expected to climb alone.

[story 2]

I found myself tracing the outline of the university map on my desk, the lines of roads and campus buildings blurring into meaningless geography. When I finally looked up, the silence of my apartment was thick, pressing in on my chest. Albright was gone. It felt less like a professional transition and more like a sudden amputation. I picked up my phone, intending to call him, but the muscle memory of dialing failed me. Instead, I just scrolled through old correspondence, reading the polite acknowledgments of past meetings, each one a tiny, perfectly preserved artifact of a relationship that was now dissolving. A slow, heavy sigh escaped my lips, a sound devoid of any real effort. I let my gaze drift to the window, watching the city lights flicker on, feeling utterly unmoored, as if the very foundation of my academic trajectory had just shifted beneath my feet.

[story 3]

The coffee in my mug had gone cold, mirroring the temperature of my mood. I sat at the edge of my bed, staring at the ceiling, the plaster pattern mocking the relentless, unchanging nature of the situation. Professor Albright’s departure was a fact now, settled in the quiet hum of my mind like a permanent fixture. I didn't bother to check my email again; the knowledge was already absorbed, a cold certainty settling deep in my stomach. I stood up, moving with a deliberate slowness, as if each step required an immense expenditure of energy. I walked to my closet and pulled out a sweater, the fabric feeling heavy and unremarkable. There was no sudden panic, only a deep, pervasive sense of inevitability. The work ahead seemed impossibly vast, and I simply allowed myself to inhabit the quiet space of knowing that the path forward would require navigating entirely uncharted territory.
